entities.description: A public keyed transcription of the two poem texts selected from Kaluza 1890. It retains printed page breaks, Fillingham-transcript folio milestones in the first poem, editorial notes, line groups, and two Latin quotation blocks in the second poem, while omitting most of the article's German editorial prose.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00080

rights.basis: The current U-M Corpus of Middle English collection page states that its materials are public domain in the United States and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation
notes: The statement is jurisdiction-specific and does not determine rights in independently supplied manuscript photographs or every interface asset.

bibliographic.citation: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften,' Englische Studien 14 (1890): 165–187.
bibliographic.container_title: Englische Studien: Organ für englische Philologie
bibliographic.title: Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften

entities.description: The immediate print source. CME00080 encodes the first poem's text on pp. 171–177 and the second poem's text on pp. 184–187, not Kaluza's complete pp. 165–187 article.
entities.label: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften' (1890)
notes: Section I is headed 'The Eremyte and the Outelawe' and identifies Add MS 22577; Section II is headed 'Die Boten des Todes' and identifies Add MS 22283.
Kaluza's statements that both poems were previously unpublished were already false in 1890: Park printed the first in 1816 and Horstmann the second in 1887.

rights.basis: Anonymous medieval works first published no later than 1816 and 1887.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval poem texts
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editorial text, XML markup, photographs, or provider-delivered files.

rights.basis: First published in 1890, before 1930.
rights.component: 1890 scholarly edition and commentary

access.accessMethod: Open the exact British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.

localCopies.label: British Library Add MS 22577 catalog snapshot

access.accessMethod: No public complete page-image object was found. The exact catalog exposes no Digitised Content, and Add MS 22577 is absent from the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list.

Use the British Library Imaging Services form. Request the complete current paper object—covers, spine, boards or pastedowns if applicable, flyleaves, blanks, and every recto and verso—and identify The Hermit and the Outlaw separately as the full transcript, associated by Kaluza with folio milestones 3a–15a. Ask the Library to confirm current foliation and complete-object extent.
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services; permissions@bl.uk for written reuse permission
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography; exact whole-object quote subject to repository review

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

entities.description: The c. 1806 paper working transcript used directly by Kaluza for The Hermit and the Outlaw. William Fillingham copied it from the medieval manuscript now Add MS 37492 and presented it to Philip Bliss.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 22577

physicalDescription.extent: 1 item; exact leaf count not stated in the current public record
notes: The catalog separates 363 narrative lines from a preceding, miscounted 23-line introduction; Kaluza establishes that the introduction has 24 lines and is integral.
Kaluza's transcription includes folio milestones from 3a through 15a, but their exact concordance with current BL foliation has not been visually verified.

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

access.accessMethod: Open the exact British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.

localCopies.label: British Library Add MS 37492 catalog snapshot

access.accessMethod: No public complete page-image object was found. The exact catalog exposes no Digitised Content, and Add MS 37492 is absent from the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list.

Use the British Library Imaging Services form. Request the complete current codex—covers, spine, boards, pastedowns, four preliminary leaves, blanks, every recto and verso through fol. 92, and edges where offered—in colour. Identify The Hermit and the Outlaw separately at ff. 76v–82v.
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services; permissions@bl.uk for written reuse permission
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography; exact whole-object quote subject to repository review

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

entities.description: The surviving medieval source of The Hermit and the Outlaw, copied by J. Gage and formerly owned by William Fillingham. The poem occupies ff. 76v–82v within a larger paper volume of romantic and religious poems.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 37492

physicalDescription.format: Codex of romantic and religious poems
notes: Written by J. Gage.
The present object was in William Fillingham's possession in 1805; the current catalog identifies it confidently with the source described as vellum in Bliss's note to Add MS 22577.

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

entities.description: A transcript that Thomas Park says William Fillingham extracted from his manuscript volume and allowed Park to use. The current Add MS 37492 catalog calls it a transcript of that manuscript and distinguishes it from Add MS 22577, another Fillingham transcript.

holding.notes: No current shelfmark or survival evidence was found. This entity uses the manuscript_witness schema type provisionally for a material working transcript.

entities.label: Fillingham transcript used for Park's 1816 edition
notes: Do not identify this physical transcript with Add MS 22577 without further provenance evidence.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Park, ed., 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe. A Balade,' in Sir Egerton Brydges, Restituta; or, Titles, Extracts, and Characters of Old Books in English Literature, Revived, vol. 4 (London: T. Bensley for Longman, Hurst, Rees, Orme, and Brown, 1816), 91–104.
bibliographic.container_title: Restituta; or, Titles, Extracts, and Characters of Old Books in English Literature, Revived
bibliographic.publisher: T. Bensley for Longman, Hurst, Rees, Orme, and Brown
bibliographic.title: The Eremyte and the Owtelawe. A Balade

entities.description: The first known complete publication of the poem, including its 24-line opening before 'Off tweye brethyrn.' Park printed from a Fillingham transcript and retained acknowledged imperfections rather than conjecturally supplying them.
entities.label: Thomas Park, 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe' (1816)

rights.basis: First published in 1816, before 1930.
rights.component: 1816 edition

access.accessMethod: Open the exact British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.

localCopies.label: British Library Add MS 22283 catalog snapshot

access.accessMethod: The exact catalog links a legacy Digitised Manuscripts record but explicitly states that its digital images are currently unavailable; Add MS 22283 is absent from the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list.

Use the British Library Imaging Services form. Request the complete current parchment codex—covers, spine, boards, pastedowns, all unfoliated modern leaves, every recto and verso through fol. 172, all five inserted leaves, blanks, and edges where offered—in colour. Identify Three Messengers of Death separately at ff. 88vb–89ra.
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services; permissions@bl.uk for written reuse permission
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography; exact whole-object quote subject to repository review
notes: A letter of introduction is required for onsite consultation, but the Imaging Services form is the current mediated reproduction route.

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

entities.description: The late-fourteenth-century parchment codex directly transcribed by Kaluza for Three Messengers of Death. The poem occupies ff. 88vb–89ra and shares its terminal column with the following Song of Love-Longing to Jesus.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: 172 folios, plus five unfoliated modern paper leaves at the beginning, 24 at the end, and five inserted between specified folio pairs
notes: The manuscript is closely associated in production and contents with the Vernon Manuscript.
Its current record requires a letter of introduction for onsite consultation.

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms prohibit copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.
rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

entities.description: The parallel late-fourteenth-century Vernon witness of Three Messengers of Death at ff. 297vc–298rb. Kaluza examined it cursorily for variants; Horstmann and the modern METS edition use it as their base.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Eng. poet. a.1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves; incomplete, originally viii + 413 with 71 leaves lost
physicalDescription.format: Large, richly decorated double- and triple-column devotional codex
notes: Digital Bodleian gives England, West Midlands? as the place of origin and describes the manuscript as an extensive collection of Middle English religious and didactic texts.
The work begins partway down the third column of fol. 297v and ends in the second column of fol. 298r, with adjacent works on both surfaces.

next_steps: Trace Park's papers and Fillingham provenance records.
Compare Park's readings with Add MS 22577 and Add MS 37492 before proposing physical identity.
openQuestions.question: Does the separate Fillingham transcript used by Thomas Park survive, and can it be identified with any current shelfmark?

next_steps: Ask British Library Imaging Services to confirm the complete leaf count and current recto/verso labels before ordering.
Crosswalk Kaluza's 3a–15a milestones to the delivered images without assuming a modern foliation.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current foliation and whole-object surface inventory of Add MS 22577?

next_steps: Monitor the exact catalog's Digitised Content field and the provider's digitised-manuscript list.
Meanwhile request a whole-object color reproduction and written reuse terms through Imaging Services.
openQuestions.question: Will the British Library restore public access to the legacy complete images for Add MS 22283?

next_steps: Submit separate whole-object quote requests using the exact surface scopes in each access record.
Ask permissions@bl.uk for explicit written terms covering repository publication, scholarship, and any commercial print-on-demand reuse before redistributing supplied images.
notes: Published per-image rates are not a reliable total for these large, fragile objects; the current terms make large orders subject to individual discussion.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color research facsimiles and publication permission be obtained for Add MSS 22577, 37492, and 22283 at a practical cost?

next_steps: Consult Green's complete 2003 edition and current exemplum scholarship.
Do not turn Kaluza's general Latin-or-French-source conjecture into an upstream lineage entity without a specific textual identification.
openQuestions.question: What specific source tradition or analogue underlies The Hermit and the Outlaw?

reviewNotes: The two poems are separate intellectual works and every witness or edition relation is part-scoped.
Park 1816 and Horstmann 1887 are prior editions, not sources of Kaluza's readings.
The complete Vernon facsimile is cached and mapped to the exact columns; the British Library negative findings are explicitly dated and requestable rather than treated as evidence of loss.

No new reusable modeling problem was found beyond the skill's existing rules for edited selections, intermediary transcripts, lineation differences, complete-codex caching, and provider-specific image rights.

lineage.summary: CME00080 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of two non-contiguous poem texts from Max Kaluza's 1890 article 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften.' The Hermit and the Outlaw (Kaluza pp. 171–177) was transcribed from British Library Add MS 22577, William Fillingham's c. 1806 paper transcript of the surviving fifteenth-century source now Add MS 37492, ff. 76v–82v. Thomas Park had already printed the complete poem, including its 24-line prologue, from a separate Fillingham transcript in 1816. Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza pp. 184–187) was transcribed from the late-fourteenth-century Simeon Manuscript, BL Add MS 22283, ff. 88vb–89ra, with limited comparison against the parallel Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian MS Eng. poet. a.1, ff. 297vc–298rb. Horstmann had printed the Vernon form in 1887. Complete scans of the three historical print volumes, the complete DIMEV 1.0 release, a complete modern METS edition, and Digital Bodleian's complete 710-canvas Vernon project are cached locally. The full Vernon bundle retains covers, endleaves, and every exposed manuscript surface; one-based canvases 481–482 map the work. None of the three relevant British Library objects currently has an obtainable complete public facsimile: Add MS 22283's legacy images are explicitly unavailable, and Add MSS 22577 and 37492 expose catalog records but no Digitised Content. Whole-object Imaging Services request routes and image-reuse restrictions are recorded separately.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: The poems are tentatively dated by Kaluza to the fourteenth century; surviving medieval witnesses are late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century; first published separately in 1816 and 1887; Kaluza selection published 1890
catalogSummary.region: English Midlands dialect for both poems; Three Messengers of Death is localized more specifically to the West Midlands
catalogSummary.title: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)

catalogingSubject.label: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the two-poem Middle English selection encoded in CME00080 from Max Kaluza's 1890 article: the complete texts of The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death, with textual notes embedded in the transcription but without most of Kaluza's German introductions and commentary. The poems are independent anonymous works with different witness histories; the unit is not one medieval work, the complete article, or any complete manuscript.

contentParts.label: The Hermit and the Outlaw

locators.value: Kaluza's milestones 3a–15a; exact current recto/verso concordance unverified

notes: Kaluza treats the first 24 lines as integral although the Add MS 22577 catalog separates and miscounts them as 23 lines.
Several stanzas are incomplete and a larger textual lacuna follows Kaluza line 99; the 34 XML line groups are encoding containers, not 34 complete twelve-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A religious exemplum in which an outlaw repents, accepts a seemingly small penance not to drink water, dies rather than break it, and is carried to heaven; an angel explains his salvation to his hermit brother.

contentParts.label: Three Messengers of Death

locators.value: DIV1 2; 54 LG, two Q blocks, and 220 L

notes: The XML has 54 four-line Middle English LGs (216 lines) plus two Latin Q blocks encoded as two long lines each, for 220 L total.
Cook and Strakhov instead print each Latin distich as a four-line quatrain and describe 56 quatrains / 224 lines. This is a lineation difference, not missing text in the XML.
contentParts.summary: A macaronic verse homily presenting Chance, Sickness, and Old Age as Death's three messengers and urging confession, repentance, and preparation for judgment.

assertion.notes: Kaluza inferred the range from a general plague allusion; a modern composition-date review remains needed.

dateStatements.display: Tentatively third quarter of the fourteenth century in Kaluza's plague-based dating
Surviving medieval witness: second half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The modern METS introduction confirms late-fourteenth-century witnesses but does not validate Kaluza's earlier composition estimate.

dateStatements.display: Tentatively first half of the fourteenth century in Kaluza's dating
Earliest surviving attestation window: late fourteenth century; Simeon is dated 1360–1399 and both Simeon and Vernon are commonly described as c. 1390–1400
notes: The normalized range combines the British Library's 1360–1399 range for Simeon with METS's c. 1390–1400 description of both witnesses; it is an attestation window, not a composition range or proof that one surviving copy is physically earliest.

assertion.notes: Park printed the prologue and narrative on pp. 91–104; Kaluza's 1890 claim that the poem was unpublished was mistaken.
Horstmann printed the Vernon version on pp. 432–434 before Kaluza's 1890 Simeon-based edition.

dateStatements.display: 1890 as this exact two-poem Kaluza selection

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 work containers. Part 1 has 34 LG and 387 L; part 2 has 54 LG, two Q blocks, and 220 L. The 88 LG and 607 L totals describe the encoding, not a normalized stanza or verse count across editorial systems.

formStatements.description: Two independent verse works selected from one article; brief rubrics and embedded Latin do not change the whole-unit form.

scope.description: Two embedded Latin distichs encoded in Q blocks

notes: The preferred whole-unit title is supplied because CME00080 has no encoded title spanning both poem divisions.
London and Oxford are holding places and Leipzig, London, and Braunschweig are publication places; none is used as a place-of-composition claim.
Kaluza's historical statements are preserved as attributed claims rather than silently modernized.

next_steps: Consult Richard Firth Green's modern edition and current scholarship on The Hermit and the Outlaw.
Review linguistic, metrical, and source studies of Three Messengers of Death independently of witness dates.
openQuestions.question: What composition dates can current scholarship support for each poem beyond Kaluza's nineteenth-century conjectures?

next_steps: Search modern motif, exemplum, and sermon indexes and Green's textual introduction.
Do not create a source-work lineage entity until a specific analogue and relationship are evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Can Kaluza's proposed but unidentified Latin or French source for The Hermit and the Outlaw be identified?

next_steps: Retain both attributed counts and explain that METS divides each of four Latin hexameters into paired half-lines.
openQuestions.question: Should corpus UI expose both the CME 220-line metric and METS 224-line lineation for Three Messengers of Death?

assertion.notes: Kaluza's broad dialect conclusion; not proof of place of composition or of the fifteenth-century witness's production.
METS identifies the poem with the West Midlands language of the Vernon Manuscript; this is linguistic localization, not a demonstrated place of composition.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of The Hermit and the Outlaw
Anonymous poet of Three Messengers of Death

subjects.label: Chance, sickness, and old age as messengers of death

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English religious poems as edited by Max Kaluza in 1890. The Hermit and the Outlaw narrates an outlaw's conversion, extreme fidelity to a small penance, death, and salvation. Three Messengers of Death is a macaronic verse homily in which Chance, Sickness, and Old Age warn humanity of death and judgment. CME00080 preserves the poem texts and textual annotations but omits most of the article's German editorial discussion.

The 607 XML L elements are encoding metrics: 387 in The Hermit and the Outlaw and 220 in Three Messengers. In the latter, 216 Middle English lines are grouped in 54 LGs and four long Latin lines in two Q blocks; the modern METS edition instead divides the Latin into eight short lines and reports 56 quatrains / 224 lines.

assertion.notes: A supplied title that makes the two-work selection boundary explicit.

titles.value: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)

scope.description: The encompassing article title; CME00080 includes only its two poem texts rather than the complete article.

assertion.notes: Kaluza's section heading and Fillingham's designation; it is not asserted here as a rubric visible in the medieval witness. Spelling and punctuation vary across sources.

titles.value: The Eremyte and the Outelawe. A Balade.

The Hermit and the Outlaw narrates an outlaw's conversion, extreme fidelity to a small penance, death, and salvation. Three Messengers of Death is a macaronic verse homily in which Chance, Sickness, and Old Age warn humanity of death and judgment. CME00080 preserves the poem texts and textual annotations but omits most of the article's German editorial discussion.
